channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Ionic glass formers show an inverted relation between fragility and non-exponential alpha-relaxation
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 08 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-025-68124-2

An empirical correlation between the fragility of glass forming liquids and the broadness of their relaxation spectrum is believed to be universal. Van Lange et al. report an inverted correlation in a class of polymeric materials, implying a special role of long-ranged ionic interactions in vitrification.
item.dc:title: Ionic glass formers show an inverted relation between fragility and non-exponential alpha-relaxation
item.dc:creator: Sophie G. M. van Lange
Diane W. te Brake
Eline F. Brink
Jochem Pees
Mathilde M. van Nieuwenhuijzen
Nayan Vengallur
Alessio Zaccone
Andrea Giuntoli
Joris Sprakel
Jasper van der Gucht

item.title: Bulk-cusp microstructure for controllable multi-directional liquid spreading
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 08 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-025-68237-8

The ability to control the wetting of liquids on solid surfaces is crucial for advanced science and engineering. Here, a bulk-cusp microstructure enables programmable 0–4 directional droplet spreading without external energy, driven by precursor film dynamics and capillary forces, offering potential for improved lubrication and smart cooling applications.
item.dc:title: Bulk-cusp microstructure for controllable multi-directional liquid spreading
item.dc:creator: Songjie Dai
Hui Zhang
Yang Liu
Fenghao Yi
Kaibin Shi
Guangneng Dong
